chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, straight wire of radius $r$ carrying a uniformly distributed current $i$ is positioned horizontally in a uniform magnetic field $B$ that is directed vertically upward. What will be the minimum magnitude of the resultant magnetic field?
options: $0$ if $r \ge \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi B}$, and $B - \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$ if $r < \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi B}$
$0$ if $r \le \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi B}$, and $B - \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$ if $r > \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi B}$
$B - \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$ for all possible values of $r$
questions.solution:
The external magnetic field is $B$ directed vertically upward.

The magnetic field due to the current-carrying wire forms concentric circles. In the horizontal plane passing through the axis of the wire, the magnetic field is vertical. On one side of the wire, it is directed upward, and on the other side, it is directed downward.

The resultant magnetic field will have a minimum magnitude on the side where the wire's magnetic field is directed downward, opposing the external field.

The magnitude of the magnetic field due to the wire at a distance $x$ from its axis is $B_{wire} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i x}{2\pi r^2}$ for $x \le r$ and $B_{wire} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x}$ for $x \ge r$.

The maximum value of $B_{wire}$ occurs at the surface of the wire ($x = r$), which is $B_{max} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$.

If $B_{max} \ge B$, there exists a point where $B_{wire} = B$. At this point, the resultant magnetic field is zero. This happens when $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r} \ge B \Rightarrow r \le \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi B}$.

If $B_{max} < B$, the maximum downward field the wire can produce is $B_{max}$. The minimum resultant magnetic field will be $B - B_{max} = B - \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$. This happens when $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r} < B \Rightarrow r > \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi B}$.

Therefore, the minimum magnitude is $0$ if $r \le \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi B}$, and $B - \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$ if $r > \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi B}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, straight wire carrying a $30 \text{ A}$ current is situated in a uniform external magnetic field of $4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ that is directed parallel to the current. Determine the magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at a location $2.0 \text{ cm}$ away from the wire.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the long straight wire at a distance $r = 2.0 \text{ cm}$ is given by:

$B_{wire} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}$

Substituting the given values:

$B_{wire} = \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 30}{2 \pi \times 0.02}$

$B_{wire} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1500 = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

The direction of this magnetic field is perpendicular to the wire. The external magnetic field is parallel to the current in the wire, so it is perpendicular to the magnetic field produced by the wire.

The magnitude of the external magnetic field is $B_{ext} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$.

Since the two magnetic fields are perpendicular to each other, the resultant magnetic field is:

$B_{net} = \sqrt{B_{wire}^2 + B_{ext}^2}$

$B_{net} = \sqrt{(3.0 \times 10^{-4})^2 + (4.0 \times 10^{-4})^2}$

$B_{net} = \sqrt{9.0 \times 10^{-8} + 16.0 \times 10^{-8}}$

$B_{net} = \sqrt{25.0 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, straight wire carrying a current of $1.0 \text{ A}$ is positioned horizontally within a uniform magnetic field $B = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$ that points vertically upward, as shown in the figure. Determine the magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at points $P$ and $Q$, which are both located at a distance of $2.0 \text{ cm}$ from the wire in the same horizontal plane.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by a long straight current-carrying wire at a distance $d$ is given by:

$B_{\text{wire}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi d}$

Given $i = 1.0 \text{ A}$ and $d = 2.0 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$, we get:

$B_{\text{wire}} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.0}{2\pi \times 0.02} = 10^{-5} \text{ T} = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

According to the right-hand thumb rule, the direction of the magnetic field due to the wire at point $P$ (above the wire) is outwards ($\odot$), and at point $Q$ (below the wire) is inwards ($\otimes$).

The external uniform magnetic field is $B = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T} = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$, directed outwards ($\odot$).

At point $P$, both magnetic fields are in the same direction. The resultant magnetic field is:

$B_P = B_{\text{wire}} + B = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{T} + 10 \text{ }\mu\text{T} = 20 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

At point $Q$, the magnetic fields are in opposite directions. The resultant magnetic field is:

$B_Q = B_{\text{wire}} - B = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{T} - 10 \text{ }\mu\text{T} = 0$

Therefore, the resultant magnetic fields at $P$ and $Q$ are $20 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$ and zero, respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Determine the maximum magnitude of the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ produced by a copper wire having a diameter of $1.6 \text{ mm}$ and carrying a current of $20 \text{ A}$.
questions.solution:
Given:
Diameter of the wire, $d = 1.6 \text{ mm}$
Radius of the wire, $R = \dfrac{d}{2} = 0.8 \text{ mm} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
Current, $I = 20 \text{ A}$

The magnetic field produced by a current-carrying wire is maximum at its surface.
Using Ampere's law, the maximum magnetic field is given by:
$B_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi R}$

Substituting the given values:
$B_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 20}{2 \pi \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}}$

$B_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 20}{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}$

$B_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-7}}{8 \times 10^{-4}}$

$B_{\text{max}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Using the formulae $\vec{F} = q \vec{v} \times \vec{B}$ and $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$, determine the SI units of the magnetic field $B$ and the permeability constant $\mu_0$ respectively.
questions.solution:
From the formula $\vec{F} = q \vec{v} \times \vec{B}$, the magnitude of the magnetic field is given by $B = \dfrac{F}{qv\sin\theta}$.

Substituting the SI units, the unit of $B$ is $\dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{C} \cdot \text{m s}^{-1}}$.

Since $\text{C s}^{-1} = \text{A}$, the unit of $B$ is $\text{N A}^{-1} \text{m}^{-1}$.

From the formula $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$, the permeability constant is given by $\mu_0 = \dfrac{2\pi r B}{i}$.

Substituting the SI units, the unit of $\mu_0$ is $\dfrac{\text{m} \cdot \text{N A}^{-1} \text{m}^{-1}}{\text{A}} = \text{N A}^{-2}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two long, straight wires are positioned along the $x$- and $y$-axes, respectively, with each carrying a current of $5 \text{ A}$. The currents are directed along the positive directions of the axes. Determine the magnetic field at the point $(1 \text{ m}, 1 \text{ m})$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to a long straight wire carrying current $I$ at a perpendicular distance $d$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}$.

For the wire along the $x$-axis carrying current in the positive $x$-direction, the magnetic field at $(1 \text{ m}, 1 \text{ m})$ is directed along the positive $z$-axis (using the right-hand thumb rule).

$\vec{B}_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi y} \hat{k} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 1} \hat{k} = 10^{-6} \hat{k} \text{ T} = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

For the wire along the $y$-axis carrying current in the positive $y$-direction, the magnetic field at $(1 \text{ m}, 1 \text{ m})$ is directed along the negative $z$-axis.

$\vec{B}_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi x} (-\hat{k}) = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 1} (-\hat{k}) = -10^{-6} \hat{k} \text{ T} = -1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

The net magnetic field at the point $(1 \text{ m}, 1 \text{ m})$ is the vector sum of the two fields:

$\vec{B}_{net} = \vec{B}_1 + \vec{B}_2 = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k} - 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k} = 0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two long, straight wires are positioned along the $x$- and $y$-axes, respectively, with each carrying a current of $5 \text{ A}$. The currents are directed along the positive directions of the axes. Determine the magnetic field at the point $(-1 \text{ m}, 1 \text{ m})$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to an infinitely long straight wire carrying current $I$ at a perpendicular distance $d$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}$.

For the wire along the $x$-axis, the current is in the $+\hat{i}$ direction. The point $(-1, 1)$ is at a perpendicular distance $d_1 = 1 \text{ m}$ from the $x$-axis. Using the right-hand thumb rule, the magnetic field $\vec{B}_1$ at this point is directed along the positive $z$-axis ($+\hat{k}$).

$\vec{B}_1 = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 1} \hat{k} = 10^{-6} \hat{k} \text{ T} = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

For the wire along the $y$-axis, the current is in the $+\hat{j}$ direction. The point $(-1, 1)$ is at a perpendicular distance $d_2 = |-1| = 1 \text{ m}$ from the $y$-axis. Using the right-hand thumb rule, the magnetic field $\vec{B}_2$ at this point is also directed along the positive $z$-axis ($+\hat{k}$).

$\vec{B}_2 = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 1} \hat{k} = 10^{-6} \hat{k} \text{ T} = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

The net magnetic field at the point $(-1, 1)$ is the vector sum of the individual magnetic fields:

$\vec{B} = \vec{B}_1 + \vec{B}_2 = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k} + 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k} = 2 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

Thus, the net magnetic field is $2 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$ along the positive $z$-axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two long, straight wires are positioned along the $x$- and $y$-axes, respectively, with each carrying a current of $5 \text{ A}$. The currents are directed along the positive directions of the axes. Determine the magnetic field at the point $(-1 \text{ m}, -1 \text{ m})$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to a long straight wire carrying current $I$ at a perpendicular distance $d$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi d}$.

For the wire along the $x$-axis, the current is in the $+\hat{i}$ direction. The position vector of the point $(-1, -1)$ with respect to the origin on the $x$-axis is in the $-\hat{j}$ direction (since $y = -1 \text{ m}$).

The magnetic field $\vec{B}_1$ due to this wire is:

$\vec{B}_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi d} (\hat{i} \times -\hat{j}) = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 1} (-\hat{k}) = -10^{-6} \hat{k} \text{ T} = -1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

For the wire along the $y$-axis, the current is in the $+\hat{j}$ direction. The position vector of the point with respect to the $y$-axis is in the $-\hat{i}$ direction (since $x = -1 \text{ m}$).

The magnetic field $\vec{B}_2$ due to this wire is:

$\vec{B}_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi d} (\hat{j} \times -\hat{i}) = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 1} (\hat{k}) = 10^{-6} \hat{k} \text{ T} = 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

The net magnetic field at the point $(-1 \text{ m}, -1 \text{ m})$ is the vector sum of the individual fields:

$\vec{B}_{\text{net}} = \vec{B}_1 + \vec{B}_2 = -1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k} + 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k} = 0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two long, straight wires are positioned along the $x$- and $y$-axes, respectively, with each carrying a current of $5 \text{ A}$. The currents are directed along the positive directions of the axes. Determine the magnetic field at the point $(1 \text{ m}, -1 \text{ m})$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to a long straight wire carrying current $I$ at a perpendicular distance $r$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$.

For the wire along the $x$-axis, the current $I_1 = 5 \text{ A}$ flows in the $+\hat{i}$ direction. The point $(1 \text{ m}, -1 \text{ m})$ is at a perpendicular distance $r_1 = 1 \text{ m}$ from the $x$-axis.

Using the right-hand rule, the magnetic field $\vec{B}_1$ at this point is directed into the plane (negative $z$-axis).

$\vec{B}_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi r_1} (-\hat{k}) = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 1} (-\hat{k}) = -1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

For the wire along the $y$-axis, the current $I_2 = 5 \text{ A}$ flows in the $+\hat{j}$ direction. The point $(1 \text{ m}, -1 \text{ m})$ is at a perpendicular distance $r_2 = 1 \text{ m}$ from the $y$-axis.

Using the right-hand rule, the magnetic field $\vec{B}_2$ at this point is also directed into the plane (negative $z$-axis).

$\vec{B}_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_2}{2\pi r_2} (-\hat{k}) = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2\pi \times 1} (-\hat{k}) = -1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$

The net magnetic field is $\vec{B} = \vec{B}_1 + \vec{B}_2 = -1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k} - 1 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k} = -2 \text{ }\mu\text{T} \hat{k}$.

The magnetic field is $2 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$ along the negative $z$-axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Four long, straight wires, each carrying a current of $5.0 \text{ A}$, are arranged in a plane as depicted in the figure. Their points of intersection create a square with a side length of $5.0 \text{ cm}$. Determine the magnetic field at the centre $P$ of the square.
questions.solution:
Let the side length of the square be $a = 5.0 \text{ cm}$ and the current in each wire be $I = 5.0 \text{ A}$.

The center $P$ of the square is at a perpendicular distance of $a/2$ from each of the four long, straight wires.

Using the right-hand grip rule, the direction of the magnetic field produced by each wire at point $P$ can be determined:

The top horizontal wire carries current to the right, producing a magnetic field at $P$ directed into the plane of the paper.

The bottom horizontal wire carries current to the right, producing a magnetic field at $P$ directed out of the plane of the paper.

The left vertical wire carries current upwards, producing a magnetic field at $P$ directed into the plane of the paper.

The right vertical wire carries current upwards, producing a magnetic field at $P$ directed out of the plane of the paper.

The magnitude of the magnetic field due to each long wire at a distance $a/2$ is $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi (a/2)} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a}$.

Since the magnetic fields from the top and bottom wires are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, their net contribution at $P$ is zero.

Similarly, the magnetic fields from the left and right wires are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction, so their net contribution is also zero.

Therefore, the total magnetic field at the center $P$ is zero.

Answer: zero

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Four long, straight wires, each carrying a current of $5.0 \text{ A}$, are arranged in a plane. Their points of intersection create a square with a side length of $5.0 \text{ cm}$, with centre $P$. Points $Q_1$, $Q_2$, $Q_3$, and $Q_4$ are located on the diagonals of this square at a distance from $P$ equal to the length of the square's diagonal, as shown in the figure. Determine the magnetic fields at these four points.
questions.solution:
Let the centre of the square be the origin $(0,0)$. The side length of the square is $a = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$.

The four wires are located at $x = -a/2$, $x = a/2$, $y = -a/2$, and $y = a/2$. The currents are $I = 5.0 \text{ A}$ in the $+y$ direction for the vertical wires and in the $+x$ direction for the horizontal wires.

The diagonal of the square is $d = a\sqrt{2}$. The points $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3, Q_4$ are at a distance $d$ from the origin along the diagonals. Their coordinates are:
$Q_1 = (-a, a)$
$Q_2 = (a, a)$
$Q_3 = (a, -a)$
$Q_4 = (-a, -a)$

The magnetic field due to a long straight wire is $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$.

At $Q_1(-a, a)$:
Field due to wire at $y = a/2$: $B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi (a - a/2)} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a}$ ($\odot$)
Field due to wire at $y = -a/2$: $B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi (a - (-a/2))} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a}$ ($\odot$)
Field due to wire at $x = -a/2$: $B_3 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi |-a - (-a/2)|} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a}$ ($\odot$)
Field due to wire at $x = a/2$: $B_4 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi |-a - a/2|} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a}$ ($\odot$)
Total field at $Q_1$: $B_{Q1} = B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4 = \dfrac{8\mu_0 I}{3\pi a}$ ($\odot$)
Substituting the values: $B_{Q1} = \dfrac{8 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{3\pi \times 0.05} = 1.067 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T} \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ ($\odot$)

At $Q_2(a, a)$:
Fields from horizontal wires are $\odot$ with magnitudes $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a}$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a}$.
Fields from vertical wires are $\otimes$ with magnitudes $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a}$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a}$.
Total field at $Q_2$: $B_{Q2} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a} + \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a} - \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a} - \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a} = 0$

At $Q_3(a, -a)$:
All four wires produce a magnetic field into the page ($\otimes$).
Total field at $Q_3$: $B_{Q3} = \dfrac{8\mu_0 I}{3\pi a}$ ($\otimes$) $\approx 1.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$ ($\otimes$)

At $Q_4(-a, -a)$:
Fields from horizontal wires are $\otimes$ with magnitudes $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a}$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a}$.
Fields from vertical wires are $\odot$ with magnitudes $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a}$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a}$.
Total field at $Q_4$: $B_{Q4} = -\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a} - \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a} + \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi a} + \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{3\pi a} = 0$

Answer: $Q_1$: $1.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$, $\odot$; $Q_2$: zero; $Q_3$: $1.1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$, $\otimes$; $Q_4$: zero

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a long wire is bent at its middle to form a right angle. The magnitudes of the magnetic fields at the points $P$, $Q$, $R$, and $S$ are equal. Determine this common magnitude.
questions.solution:
Let the bend of the wire be at the origin $(0,0)$.

The wire consists of two semi-infinite segments:
1. A vertical segment along the negative y-axis.
2. A horizontal segment along the negative x-axis.

The given points are $P(0, -d)$, $Q(d, 0)$, $R(0, d)$, and $S(-d, 0)$.

Consider the magnetic field at point $P(0, -d)$:
Point $P$ lies on the vertical segment, so the magnetic field due to this segment at $P$ is zero.
Point $P$ is at a perpendicular distance $d$ from the end of the semi-infinite horizontal segment.
The magnetic field due to a semi-infinite wire at a perpendicular distance $d$ from its end is $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d}$.
Thus, the total magnetic field at $P$ is $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d}$.

Similarly, point $Q(d, 0)$ lies on the extended axis of the horizontal segment (zero contribution) and is at a perpendicular distance $d$ from the end of the vertical semi-infinite segment. The field at $Q$ is also $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d}$.

By symmetry, the fields at $R$ and $S$ have the same magnitude.

The common magnitude is $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d}$.

Answer: $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A straight wire piece of length $x$ carries a current $i$. A point $P$ is located on the perpendicular bisector of this piece, at a distance $d$ from the middle point. How does the magnetic field at point $P$ vary with distance $d$ in the limits $d \gg x$ and $d \ll x$, respectively?
options: As $1/d^2$ for both $d \gg x$ and $d \ll x$
As $1/d$ for $d \gg x$, and as $1/d^2$ for $d \ll x$
As $1/d^2$ for $d \gg x$, and as $1/d$ for $d \ll x$
As $1/d^3$ for $d \gg x$, and as $1/d^2$ for $d \ll x$
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at a point on the perpendicular bisector of a finite straight wire of length $x$ carrying current $i$ at a distance $d$ is given by Biot-Savart law:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d} (\sin\theta_1 + \sin\theta_2)$

Since the point is on the perpendicular bisector, $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \theta$. From the geometry of the figure, $\sin\theta = \dfrac{x/2}{\sqrt{d^2 + \dfrac{x^2}{4}}}$.

Substituting this into the magnetic field expression:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d} \left( \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{d^2 + \dfrac{x^2}{4}}} \right)$

For the limit $d \gg x$, the term $\dfrac{x^2}{4}$ can be neglected compared to $d^2$. Thus, $\sqrt{d^2 + \dfrac{x^2}{4}} \approx d$.

$B \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 i x}{4\pi d^2}$

Therefore, $B \propto \dfrac{1}{d^2}$ for $d \gg x$.

For the limit $d \ll x$, the term $d^2$ can be neglected compared to $\dfrac{x^2}{4}$. Thus, $\sqrt{d^2 + \dfrac{x^2}{4}} \approx \dfrac{x}{2}$.

$B \approx \dfrac{\mu_0 i x}{4\pi d \left(\dfrac{x}{2}\right)} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi d}$

Therefore, $B \propto \dfrac{1}{d}$ for $d \ll x$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A current of $10 \text{ A}$ is maintained in a long wire directed along the positive $z$-axis. Determine the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ at the point $(1 \text{ m}, 0, 0)$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to a long straight current-carrying wire is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}$

Given $I = 10 \text{ A}$ and perpendicular distance $r = 1 \text{ m}$

Substituting the values:

$B = \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{2 \pi \times 1} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T} = 2 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

The direction of the magnetic field is given by the cross product $d\vec{l} \times \vec{r}$

Since the current is along the positive $z$-axis ($d\vec{l} \parallel \hat{k}$) and the position vector is along the positive $x$-axis ($\vec{r} = \hat{i}$), the direction is along $\hat{k} \times \hat{i} = \hat{j}$

Thus, the magnetic field is $2 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$ along the positive $y$-axis

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, straight wire of radius $r$ carrying a uniformly distributed current $i$ is positioned horizontally in a uniform magnetic field $B$ that is directed vertically upward. Determine the locations where the resultant magnetic field attains its maximum magnitude, and find this maximum magnitude.
options: Looking along the direction of current, at the leftmost points on the wire's surface; $B + \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$
At points lying on the central axis of the wire; $B + \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$
Looking along the direction of current, at the topmost points on the wire's surface; $\sqrt{B^2 + \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}\right)^2}$
Looking along the direction of current, at the rightmost points on the wire's surface; $B + \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$
questions.solution:
Let the observer look along the direction of the current, meaning the current is flowing away from the observer into the plane.

According to the right-hand grip rule, the magnetic field lines produced by the straight wire form concentric clockwise circles around the wire.

The external uniform magnetic field is directed vertically upward.

To obtain the maximum resultant magnetic field, the magnetic field produced by the wire must be in the same direction as the external magnetic field (i.e., vertically upward).

Looking at the clockwise magnetic field lines, the tangent to the field lines points vertically upward at the leftmost points of the wire. At the rightmost points, the field points vertically downward.

The magnitude of the magnetic field due to the wire is maximum at its surface and is given by $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$.

Thus, at the leftmost points on the wire's surface, both the external magnetic field and the wire's magnetic field are parallel and directed vertically upward. The maximum resultant magnetic field is the sum of their magnitudes:

$B_{net} = B + \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A transmission wire positioned $8 \text{ m}$ above a road carries a current of $100 \text{ A}$. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at a point on the road.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to a long straight wire at a perpendicular distance $r$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$.

Given $I = 100 \text{ A}$ and $r = 8 \text{ m}$.

Substituting the values:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100}{2\pi \times 8}$

$B = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{8}$

$B = 0.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

$B = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A square loop of edge $a$ constructed from a uniform wire is shown in the figure. A current $i$ enters the loop at point $A$ and exits at point $C$. Suppose instead that the current $i$ enters the loop at point $A$ and exits at point $B$. Determine the magnetic field at the centre of the loop.
questions.solution:
Let the resistance of each side of the square loop be $r$.

The current $i$ enters at $A$ and exits at $B$. It divides into two parallel paths: the direct path $AB$ and the path $ADCB$.

The resistance of path $AB$ is $r$, and the resistance of path $ADCB$ is $3r$.

The current $i_1$ through $AB$ is $i_1 = i \\left( \\dfrac{3r}{r + 3r} \\right) = \\dfrac{3i}{4}$.

The current $i_2$ through $ADCB$ is $i_2 = i \\left( \\dfrac{r}{r + 3r} \\right) = \\dfrac{i}{4}$.

The magnetic field at the centre of the square due to a single wire of length $a$ carrying current $I$ is $B = \\dfrac{\\mu_0 I}{4\\pi (a/2)} (\\sin 45^\\circ + \\sin 45^\\circ) = \\dfrac{\\sqrt{2} \\mu_0 I}{\\pi a}$.

The magnetic field at the centre due to the segment $AB$ is $B_1 = \\dfrac{\\sqrt{2} \\mu_0}{\\pi a} \\left(\\dfrac{3i}{4}\\right)$. According to the right-hand thumb rule, this field is directed outwards from the plane of the loop.

The segments $AD$, $DC$, and $CB$ each carry current $i_2 = \\dfrac{i}{4}$ in the same sense, so their magnetic fields add up and are directed inwards.

The total magnetic field due to the path $ADCB$ is $B_2 = 3 \\times \\dfrac{\\sqrt{2} \\mu_0}{\\pi a} \\left(\\dfrac{i}{4}\\right) = \\dfrac{\\sqrt{2} \\mu_0}{\\pi a} \\left(\\dfrac{3i}{4}\\right)$.

Since $B_1$ and $B_2$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, the net magnetic field at the centre of the loop is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: An equilateral triangle is formed by the wire $ABC$ as shown in the figure. Assuming the wire to be uniform, determine the magnetic field $B$ at the centre $O$ of the triangle.
options: $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi L}$, where $I$ is the total current and $L$ is the side length
$\dfrac{\sqrt{3} \mu_0 I}{2 \pi L}$, where $I$ is the total current and $L$ is the side length
$\dfrac{3 \mu_0 I}{2 \pi L}$, where $I$ is the total current and $L$ is the side length
questions.solution:
Let the total current entering at vertex $A$ be $I$. The current leaves from the midpoint $M$ of the side $BC$.

The current divides into two parallel paths: path $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow M$ and path $A \rightarrow C \rightarrow M$.

Since the wire is uniform, the resistance of each path is proportional to its length. The length of path $ABM$ is $L + L/2 = 3L/2$, and the length of path $ACM$ is $L + L/2 = 3L/2$.

As the lengths and resistances of both paths are equal, the current splits equally. The current in branch $ABM$ is $I/2$ and the current in branch $ACM$ is $I/2$.

Let $d$ be the perpendicular distance from the centroid $O$ to each side of the equilateral triangle.

The magnetic field at $O$ due to the segment $AB$ is $B_{AB} = \dfrac{\mu_0 (I/2)}{4 \pi d} (\sin 60^\circ + \sin 60^\circ)$. By the right-hand rule, its direction is into the plane.

The magnetic field at $O$ due to the segment $AC$ is $B_{AC} = \dfrac{\mu_0 (I/2)}{4 \pi d} (\sin 60^\circ + \sin 60^\circ)$. Its direction is out of the plane.

Since $B_{AB}$ and $B_{AC}$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, they cancel each other out.

The magnetic field at $O$ due to the segment $BM$ is $B_{BM} = \dfrac{\mu_0 (I/2)}{4 \pi d} (\sin 60^\circ + \sin 0^\circ)$. Its direction is out of the plane.

The magnetic field at $O$ due to the segment $CM$ is $B_{CM} = \dfrac{\mu_0 (I/2)}{4 \pi d} (\sin 60^\circ + \sin 0^\circ)$. Its direction is into the plane.

Similarly, $B_{BM}$ and $B_{CM}$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, so they also cancel each other out.

Therefore, the net magnetic field at the center $O$ of the triangle is zero.

Answer: Zero

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: An electric current $i$ is carried by a wire of length $l$ which is bent into an equilateral triangle. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the centre.
questions.solution:
Let the length of each side of the equilateral triangle be $a$. Since the total length of the wire is $l$, we have $a = \dfrac{l}{3}$.

The perpendicular distance $d$ from the centroid (center) of the equilateral triangle to any of its sides is given by:

$d = \dfrac{a}{2\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{l}{3}}{2\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{l}{6\sqrt{3}}$

The magnetic field at the center due to one side of the triangle can be found using the Biot-Savart law for a finite straight wire:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d} (\sin \theta_1 + \sin \theta_2)$

For an equilateral triangle, the line segments joining the center to the vertices subtend an angle of $120^{\circ}$ at the center. The perpendicular bisects this angle, giving $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 60^{\circ}$.

Substituting the values:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi \left(\dfrac{l}{6\sqrt{3}}\right)} (\sin 60^{\circ} + \sin 60^{\circ})$

$B_1 = \dfrac{6\sqrt{3} \mu_0 i}{4\pi l} \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$

$B_1 = \dfrac{6\sqrt{3} \mu_0 i}{4\pi l} (\sqrt{3}) = \dfrac{18 \mu_0 i}{4\pi l} = \dfrac{9 \mu_0 i}{2\pi l}$

Since the current flows in the same sense through all three sides, the magnetic field vectors due to each side point in the same direction at the center. The total magnetic field $B$ is the sum of the fields due to all three sides:

$B = 3B_1 = 3 \times \dfrac{9 \mu_0 i}{2\pi l} = \dfrac{27 \mu_0 i}{2\pi l}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: An electric current $i$ is carried by a wire of length $l$ which is bent into a square. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the centre.
questions.solution:
Let the side length of the square be $a$. Since the total length of the wire is $l$, we have $4a = l \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{l}{4}$.

The magnetic field at the centre of the square due to one of its sides is given by the formula for a finite straight wire:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d} (\sin\theta_1 + \sin\theta_2)$

The perpendicular distance from the centre to the side is $d = \dfrac{a}{2} = \dfrac{l}{8}$.

The angles subtended by the ends of the side at the centre are $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 45^\circ$.

Substituting these values into the formula:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi \left(\dfrac{l}{8}\right)} (\sin 45^\circ + \sin 45^\circ)$

$B_1 = \dfrac{2\mu_0 i}{\pi l} \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \dfrac{2\mu_0 i}{\pi l} \left(\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\mu_0 i}{\pi l}$

Since the square has four identical sides and the magnetic field due to each side points in the same direction at the centre, the total magnetic field is:

$B = 4B_1 = 4 \times \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\mu_0 i}{\pi l} = \dfrac{8\sqrt{2}\mu_0 i}{\pi l}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long wire carrying a current $i$ is bent to create a plane angle $\alpha$. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at a point located on the angle bisector at a distance $x$ from the vertex.
questions.solution:
The bent wire can be considered as two semi-infinite straight wires joined at the vertex.

Let the point on the angle bisector be $P$, located at a distance $x$ from the vertex. The perpendicular distance $d$ from $P$ to each semi-infinite wire is given by:

$d = x \sin\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$

For each semi-infinite wire, the magnetic field at $P$ is determined by the angles subtended by its ends at $P$ with respect to the perpendicular. One end extends to infinity, subtending an angle $\theta_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$. The other end is at the vertex, subtending an angle $\theta_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{2} - \dfrac{\alpha}{2}$.

The magnetic field due to one semi-infinite wire is:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d} (\sin\theta_1 + \sin\theta_2)$

Substituting the values of $d$, $\theta_1$, and $\theta_2$:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi x \sin\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)} \left[\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2} - \dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) + \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]$

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi x \sin\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)} \left(\cos\dfrac{\alpha}{2} + 1\right)$

By the right-hand rule, the magnetic fields produced by both semi-infinite segments at point $P$ point in the same direction. The total magnetic field $B$ is the sum of the fields from both wires:

$B = 2B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x \sin\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)} \left(1 + \cos\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$

Using the half-angle trigonometric identities $1 + \cos\dfrac{\alpha}{2} = 2\cos^2\dfrac{\alpha}{4}$ and $\sin\dfrac{\alpha}{2} = 2\sin\dfrac{\alpha}{4}\cos\dfrac{\alpha}{4}$:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x} \left(\dfrac{2\cos^2\dfrac{\alpha}{4}}{2\sin\dfrac{\alpha}{4}\cos\dfrac{\alpha}{4}}\right)$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x} \cot\dfrac{\alpha}{4}$

Answer: $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi x} \cot\dfrac{\alpha}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A wire of total length $2\pi r$ carrying a current $i$ is bent to form a regular polygon of $n$ sides. By taking the limit as $n \to \infty$, determine the expression for the magnetic field at the centre of the resulting circular current.
questions.solution:
Let the regular polygon have $n$ sides. The total length of the wire is $2\pi r$, so the length of each side is $a = \dfrac{2\pi r}{n}$.

The perpendicular distance from the center to any side is $d = \dfrac{a}{2} \cot\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) = \dfrac{\pi r}{n} \cot\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right)$.

The magnetic field at the center due to one side of the polygon is given by:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d} \left[ \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) + \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) \right] = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi d} \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right)$

The total magnetic field due to all $n$ sides is:

$B = n B_1 = \dfrac{n \mu_0 i}{2\pi d} \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right)$

Substituting the value of $d$:

$B = \dfrac{n \mu_0 i}{2\pi \left[ \dfrac{\pi r}{n} \cot\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) \right]} \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2r} \left( \dfrac{n}{\pi} \right)^2 \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) \tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right)$

Taking the limit as $n \to \infty$, we can substitute $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{n}$, where $\theta \to 0$:

$B = \lim_{\theta \to 0} \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2r} \left( \dfrac{\sin \theta}{\theta} \right) \left( \dfrac{\tan \theta}{\theta} \right)$

Using the standard limits $\lim_{\theta \to 0} \dfrac{\sin \theta}{\theta} = 1$ and $\lim_{\theta \to 0} \dfrac{\tan \theta}{\theta} = 1$, we get:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2r}$

Alternatively, as $n \to \infty$, the polygon approaches a circle of circumference $2\pi r$ and radius $r$. The magnetic field at the center of a circular loop is $\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2r}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A wire of total length $2\pi r$ carrying a current $i$ is bent to form a regular polygon of $n$ sides. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of this polygon.
questions.solution:
Total length of the wire is $2\pi r$.

The polygon has $n$ sides, so the length of each side is $a = \dfrac{2\pi r}{n}$.

The perpendicular distance from the centre to any side is $d = \dfrac{a}{2} \cot\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) = \dfrac{\pi r}{n} \cot\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right)$.

The magnetic field at the centre due to one side of the polygon is given by the formula for a finite straight wire:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi d} \left[ \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) + \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) \right]$

Substituting the value of $d$:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4\pi \left( \dfrac{\pi r}{n} \cot\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) \right)} \left[ 2\sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) \right]$

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i n \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) \tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right)}{2\pi^2 r}$

Since the polygon has $n$ identical sides and the magnetic field due to each side is in the same direction, the total magnetic field at the centre is:

$B = n B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i n^2 \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right) \tan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{n}\right)}{2\pi^2 r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, vertical wire carrying a $10 \text{ A}$ current in the upward direction is situated in a region where a horizontal magnetic field of magnitude $2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$ points from south to north. Determine the point at which the resultant magnetic field is zero.
questions.solution:
Let the external magnetic field be $\vec{B}_{ext}$ pointing from South to North.

For the resultant magnetic field to be zero, the magnetic field due to the current-carrying wire $\vec{B}_{wire}$ must be equal and opposite to $\vec{B}_{ext}$.

Thus, $\vec{B}_{wire}$ must point from North to South.

Using the right-hand thumb rule, for an upward current, the magnetic field points South on the West side of the wire.

Equating the magnitudes of the magnetic fields:

$B_{wire} = B_{ext}$

$\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$

Substituting the given values:

$\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{2 \pi r} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$

$\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{r} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$

$r = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}$

$r = 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.0 \text{ mm}$

The point is $1.0 \text{ mm}$ West of the wire.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: As illustrated in the figure, two parallel wires separated by a distance of $4.0 \text{ cm}$ carry equal currents of $10 \text{ A}$ in opposite directions. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at points $A_1$, $A_2$, $A_3$, and $A_4$ respectively.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to a long straight wire carrying current $I$ at a distance $r$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$.

Given $I = 10 \text{ A}$, the constant $\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{2\pi} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}\cdot\text{m}$.

Let the left wire be $W_1$ (current into the page) and the right wire be $W_2$ (current out of the page). The distance between them is $4 \text{ cm}$.

For point $A_1$:
Distance from $W_1$ is $r_1 = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$.
Distance from $W_2$ is $r_2 = 2 + 4 = 6 \text{ cm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$.
Since $A_1$ is outside the wires and the currents are in opposite directions, the magnetic fields oppose each other.
$B_{A_1} = B_1 - B_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02} - \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.06} = 10^{-4} - \dfrac{1}{3} \times 10^{-4} = \dfrac{2}{3} \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$.

For point $A_2$:
Distance from $W_1$ is $r_1 = 1 \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$.
Distance from $W_2$ is $r_2 = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ cm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$.
Since $A_2$ is between the wires, the magnetic fields are in the same direction (downwards).
$B_{A_2} = B_1 + B_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.01} + \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.03} = 2 \times 10^{-4} + \dfrac{2}{3} \times 10^{-4} = \dfrac{8}{3} \times 10^{-4} \approx 2.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$.

For point $A_3$:
Distance from $W_1$ is $r_1 = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$.
Distance from $W_2$ is $r_2 = 4 - 2 = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$.
The magnetic fields are in the same direction.
$B_{A_3} = B_1 + B_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02} + \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02} = 10^{-4} + 10^{-4} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$.

For point $A_4$:
Distance from $W_1$ is $r_1 = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2} = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ cm} = 0.02\sqrt{2} \text{ m}$.
Distance from $W_2$ is $r_2 = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2} = 2\sqrt{2} \text{ cm} = 0.02\sqrt{2} \text{ m}$.
The magnitudes of the fields are $B_1 = B_2 = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{10^{-4}}{\sqrt{2}} \text{ T}$.
The magnetic field vectors $\vec{B}_1$ and $\vec{B}_2$ are perpendicular to their respective position vectors. Since the position vectors are at $90^{\circ}$ to each other, $\vec{B}_1$ and $\vec{B}_2$ are also at $90^{\circ}$ to each other.
$B_{A_4} = \sqrt{B_1^2 + B_2^2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{10^{-4}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{10^{-4}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10^{-8}}{2} + \dfrac{10^{-8}}{2}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$.

Answer: $0.67 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$, $2.7 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$, $2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$, $1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Each battery shown in the figure has an emf equal to $5\text{ V}$. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the point $P$ for any set of values of the resistances.
options: A non-zero constant independent of the resistances
Depends on the values of the resistances
Cannot be determined without knowing the distance of point $P$
questions.solution:
Let the potential at the bottom-middle junction be $0\text{ V}$ and at the top-middle junction be $V$.

From the polarities of the batteries, the potential at the bottom-left node is $5\text{ V}$ and at the bottom-right node is $5\text{ V}$.

Similarly, the potential at the top-left node is $V + 5\text{ V}$ and at the top-right node is $V + 5\text{ V}$.

The potential difference across the left resistor is $(V + 5) - 5 = V$.

The potential difference across the middle resistor is $V - 0 = V$.

The potential difference across the right resistor is $(V + 5) - 5 = V$.

Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at the top nodes, the total current leaving through the resistors must be zero:

$\dfrac{V}{R_1} + \dfrac{V}{R_2} + \dfrac{V}{R_3} = 0$

$V \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_3} \right) = 0$

Since the resistances are positive, $V = 0$.

As the potential difference across each resistor is zero, no current flows in any branch of the circuit.

Since the current is zero everywhere, the magnetic field $B$ produced by the circuit at point $P$ is $0$.

Answer: $0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A straight, long wire carries a current of $20 \text{ A}$. A second wire carrying an equal amount of current is placed parallel to the first. If the force exerted on a $10 \text{ cm}$ length of the second wire is $2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}$, what is the separation between the wires?
questions.solution:
The force per unit length between two parallel current-carrying wires is given by $\dfrac{F}{L} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi d}$.

The total force on a length $L$ of the second wire is $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2 L}{2 \pi d}$.

Given $I_1 = 20 \text{ A}$, $I_2 = 20 \text{ A}$, $L = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, and $F = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N}$.

Substituting the values into the formula:

$2.0 \times 10^{-5} = \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 20 \times 20 \times 0.1}{2 \pi d}$

$2.0 \times 10^{-5} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 40}{d}$

$2.0 \times 10^{-5} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{d}$

$d = \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.4 \text{ m}$

$d = 40 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two parallel wires are separated by a distance of $2.0 \text{ cm}$ and carry equal currents of $10 \text{ A}$ in the same direction. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field at a point located $2.0 \text{ cm}$ away from each of the two wires.
questions.solution:
Let the two wires be at points A and B, and the observation point be P.

Since the distance between the wires is $2.0 \text{ cm}$ and point P is $2.0 \text{ cm}$ away from both wires, triangle PAB is an equilateral triangle with side length $a = 2.0 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$.

The magnitude of the magnetic field at P due to each wire is:

$B_1 = B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi a}$

Substituting the given values:

$B_1 = \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{2 \pi \times 0.02} = 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

The magnetic field vectors $\vec{B}_1$ and $\vec{B}_2$ are perpendicular to the position vectors connecting the wires to point P. Since the angle between these position vectors is $60^\circ$ and the currents are in the same direction, the angle between $\vec{B}_1$ and $\vec{B}_2$ is also $60^\circ$.

The resultant magnetic field $B_{net}$ is given by vector addition:

$B_{net} = \sqrt{B_1^2 + B_2^2 + 2 B_1 B_2 \cos 60^\circ}$

$B_{net} = \sqrt{B_1^2 + B_1^2 + 2 B_1^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)} = \sqrt{3} B_1$

$B_{net} = \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-4} \text{ T} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Three coplanar parallel wires, each carrying a current of $10 \text{ A}$ in the same direction, are positioned with a separation of $5.0 \text{ cm}$ between consecutive wires. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic force per unit length acting on the wires.
options: $6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ on the middle wire and zero on each of the outer two wires
Zero on the middle wire and $6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ on each of the outer two wires
Zero on the middle wire and $4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ on each of the outer two wires
Zero on the middle wire and $2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ on each of the outer two wires
questions.solution:
Let the three wires be 1, 2, and 3. The force per unit length between two parallel wires carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ separated by a distance $r$ is given by $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}$.

For the middle wire (wire 2), the forces exerted by wire 1 and wire 3 are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction because the currents and distances are the same. Thus, the net force on the middle wire is zero.

For wire 1, both wire 2 and wire 3 exert attractive forces in the same direction.

$F_1 = F_{12} + F_{13} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{2 \pi d} + \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{2 \pi (2d)} = \dfrac{3 \mu_0 I^2}{4 \pi d}$

Substituting the given values $I = 10 \text{ A}$ and $d = 0.05 \text{ m}$:

$F_1 = \dfrac{3 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times (10)^2}{4 \pi \times 0.05} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-5}}{0.05} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N m}^{-1}$

By symmetry, the force per unit length on wire 3 is also $6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ N m}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: The given figure displays a segment of an electric circuit. The long wires $AB$, $CD$, and $EF$ possess identical resistances. The separation between adjacent wires is $1.0 \text{ cm}$. Wires $AE$ and $BF$ have negligible resistance, and the ammeter indicates a current of $30 \text{ A}$. Determine the magnetic force per unit length acting on wire $AB$ and wire $CD$.
options: $3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ downwards on $AB$, $2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ downwards on $CD$
$3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ downwards on $AB$, zero on $CD$
$27 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ downwards on $AB$, zero on $CD$
$2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ downwards on $AB$, zero on $CD$
questions.solution:
Since the wires $AB$, $CD$, and $EF$ have identical resistances and are connected in parallel, the total current of $30 \text{ A}$ divides equally among them.

Current in each wire, $I = \dfrac{30}{3} = 10 \text{ A}$.

The magnetic force per unit length between two parallel wires carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ separated by a distance $r$ is given by $f = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi r}$. Since the currents are in the same direction, the force is attractive.

For wire $AB$, the forces due to $CD$ and $EF$ are both attractive and act downwards.

$f_{AB} = f_{AB, CD} + f_{AB, EF}$

$f_{AB} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{2 \pi d} + \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{2 \pi (2d)} = \dfrac{3 \mu_0 I^2}{4 \pi d}$

Substituting $I = 10 \text{ A}$, $d = 1.0 \text{ cm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$, and $\mu_0 = 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$:

$f_{AB} = \dfrac{3 \times 4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times (10)^2}{4 \pi \times 0.01} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N m}^{-1}$ downwards.

For wire $CD$, it experiences an upward attractive force from $AB$ and a downward attractive force from $EF$. Since the currents and distances are equal, these forces are equal and opposite.

$f_{CD} = f_{CD, EF} - f_{CD, AB} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{2 \pi d} - \dfrac{\mu_0 I^2}{2 \pi d} = 0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, straight wire is fixed horizontally, carrying a current of $50.0 \text{ A}$. A second wire having a linear mass density of $1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg m}^{-1}$ is placed parallel to and directly above this wire at a separation of $5.0 \text{ mm}$. What current must this second wire carry so that the magnetic repulsion balances its weight?
questions.solution:
Let $I_1$ be the current in the fixed wire and $I_2$ be the current in the second wire.

The magnetic force per unit length between two parallel current-carrying wires is given by:

$\dfrac{F}{L} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi d}$

For the second wire to remain suspended, the magnetic repulsion per unit length must balance its weight per unit length ($\lambda g$):

$\dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2 \pi d} = \lambda g$

Substituting the given values ($I_1 = 50.0 \text{ A}$, $d = 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $\lambda = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg m}^{-1}$, $g = 9.8 \text{ m s}^{-2}$):

$\dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 50.0 \times I_2}{2 \pi \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.8$

$\dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 50.0 \times I_2}{5.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 9.8 \times 10^{-4}$

$2 \times 10^{-3} \times I_2 = 9.8 \times 10^{-4}$

$I_2 = \dfrac{9.8 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.49 \text{ A}$

For the magnetic force to be repulsive and push the wire upwards, the currents in the two parallel wires must flow in opposite directions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A single-turn circular loop carries a current of $5.00 \text{ A}$. Determine the radius of the loop if the magnetic field $B$ at its centre is $0.200 \text{ mT}$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$

Rearranging for the radius $R$, we get

$R = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2B}$

Substituting the given values, $I = 5.00 \text{ A}$ and $B = 0.200 \text{ mT} = 2.00 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$:

$R = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.00}{2 \times 2.00 \times 10^{-4}}$

$R = \dfrac{20\pi \times 10^{-7}}{4.00 \times 10^{-4}}$

$R = 5\pi \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$R = 15.7 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.57 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A circular coil carrying current has $100$ turns and a radius of $5.0 \text{ cm}$. It generates a magnetic field of $6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$ at its centre. Determine the magnitude of the current.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of $N$ turns is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2R}$

Rearranging for current $I$:

$I = \dfrac{B \times 2R}{\mu_0 N}$

Substituting the given values, $B = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$, $R = 5.0 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $N = 100$, and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}\cdot\text{m/A}$:

$I = \dfrac{6.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 2 \times 0.05}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100}$

$I = \dfrac{6.0 \times 10^{-6}}{4\pi \times 10^{-5}}$

$I = \dfrac{0.15}{\pi} \text{ A}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$I \approx \dfrac{0.15}{3.14} \approx 0.048 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: An electron completes $3 \times 10^5$ revolutions per second in a circle of radius $0.5$ angstrom. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the circle.
questions.solution:
The equivalent current $I$ due to the revolving electron is given by:

$I = e \times f$

Substituting the given values, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ and $f = 3 \times 10^5 \text{ Hz}$:

$I = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3 \times 10^5 = 4.8 \times 10^{-14} \text{ A}$

The magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the circular path is:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2r}$

Substituting $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m/A}$ and $r = 0.5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}$:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 4.8 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-10}}$

$B = \dfrac{19.2\pi \times 10^{-21}}{10^{-10}}$

$B = 19.2\pi \times 10^{-11} \text{ T}$

$B \approx 60.3 \times 10^{-11} \text{ T} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{-10} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A conducting circular loop of radius $a$ is joined to two long, straight wires. As shown in the figure, the straight wires carry a current $i$. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of the loop.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of the loop due to the straight wires is zero because the centre lies on the extended axis of these wires.

The current $i$ divides equally into the two semicircular parts of the loop since they have equal lengths and hence equal resistance. Thus, the current in each semicircle is $i/2$.

The magnetic field at the centre due to the upper semicircle is:
$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 (i/2)}{4a}$ (directed into the plane)

The magnetic field at the centre due to the lower semicircle is:
$B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 (i/2)}{4a}$ (directed out of the plane)

Since $B_1$ and $B_2$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, the net magnetic field at the centre is:
$B_{net} = B_1 - B_2 = 0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two circular coils of radii $5.0 \text{ cm}$ and $10 \text{ cm}$ carry equal currents of $2.0 \text{ A}$. The coils have $50$ and $100$ turns respectively and are positioned so that their planes and centres coincide. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at the common centre of the coils if the currents flow in the same sense.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2r}$

For the first coil:
$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 N_1 I_1}{2r_1} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 50 \times 2.0}{2 \times 0.05} = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

For the second coil:
$B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 N_2 I_2}{2r_2} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 2.0}{2 \times 0.10} = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

Since the currents flow in the same sense, the magnetic fields are in the same direction. The net magnetic field is:
$B = B_1 + B_2 = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} + 4\pi \times 10^{-4} = 8\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two circular coils of radii $5.0 \text{ cm}$ and $10 \text{ cm}$ carry equal currents of $2.0 \text{ A}$. The coils have $50$ and $100$ turns respectively and are positioned so that their planes and centres coincide. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at the common centre of the coils if the currents flow in the opposite sense.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of $N$ turns, radius $r$, and carrying current $I$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2r}$.

For the first coil:
$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 N_1 I_1}{2r_1} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 50 \times 2.0}{2 \times 0.05} = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

For the second coil:
$B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 N_2 I_2}{2r_2} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 2.0}{2 \times 0.10} = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

Since the currents flow in the opposite sense, the magnetic fields produced by the two coils at their common centre are in opposite directions.

The net magnetic field is $B = |B_1 - B_2| = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} - 4\pi \times 10^{-4} = 0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two circular coils having radii of $5.0 \text{ cm}$ and $10 \text{ cm}$ carry equal currents of $2.0 \text{ A}$. The coils consist of $50$ and $100$ turns respectively, and they are positioned so that their centres and planes coincide. If the outer coil is subsequently rotated by $90^\circ$ about a diameter, determine the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at the centre.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of $N$ turns, radius $R$, carrying current $I$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2 R}$.

For the inner coil:
$B_1 = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 50 \times 2.0}{2 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-2}} = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

For the outer coil:
$B_2 = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 2.0}{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-2}} = 4\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

When the outer coil is rotated by $90^\circ$ about its diameter, its plane becomes perpendicular to the plane of the inner coil. Consequently, their magnetic fields at the centre will be mutually perpendicular.

The resultant magnetic field $B$ is:
$B = \sqrt{B_1^2 + B_2^2}$

$B = \sqrt{(4\pi \times 10^{-4})^2 + (4\pi \times 10^{-4})^2}$

$B = 4\pi \sqrt{2} \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

$B \approx 4 \times 3.14 \times 1.414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T} \approx 1.77 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$

Rounding to one decimal place, $B \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A circular loop of radius $R$ carries a current $I$. A second circular loop of radius $r$ ($\ll R$) carrying a current $i$ is positioned at the centre of the larger loop. The planes of the two circles are at right angles to one another. Determine the torque acting on the smaller loop.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the larger loop at its centre is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$.

The magnetic dipole moment of the smaller loop is $m = iA = i(\pi r^2)$.

Since the planes of the two loops are at right angles, the angle between the magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$ and the magnetic dipole moment vector $\vec{m}$ is $90^\circ$.

The torque acting on the smaller loop is $\tau = mB \sin 90^\circ$.

Substituting the values of $m$ and $B$, we get $\tau = (i\pi r^2) \left( \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R} \right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi i I r^2}{2R}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A circular loop of radius $r$ carrying a current $i$ is maintained at the centre of a second circular loop of radius $R(\gg r)$ which carries a current $I$. The plane of the smaller loop is oriented at an angle of $30^\circ$ relative to the plane of the larger loop. What is the minimum magnitude of a single force that must be applied at a point on the periphery of the smaller loop to hold it fixed in this position?
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by the larger loop at its centre is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}$

The magnetic moment of the smaller loop is:

$M = i A = i \pi r^2$

The angle between the plane of the smaller loop and the plane of the larger loop is $30^\circ$. Therefore, the angle between their area vectors (or between the magnetic moment $\vec{M}$ and the magnetic field $\vec{B}$) is also $\theta = 30^\circ$.

The magnitude of the torque exerted by the magnetic field on the smaller loop is:

$\tau = M B \sin \theta$

Substituting the values, we get:

$\tau = (i \pi r^2) \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}\right) \sin 30^\circ$

$\tau = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi i I r^2}{2 R} \times \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi i I r^2}{4 R}$

To hold the smaller loop fixed, an external torque of equal magnitude must be applied. This torque is provided by a single force $F$ applied at a point on the periphery of the smaller loop.

The torque due to this force about the centre is $\tau = F \times d$, where $d$ is the perpendicular distance from the centre to the line of action of the force. To minimize the required force, the distance $d$ must be maximized. The maximum possible distance for a point on the periphery is the radius of the smaller loop, $r$.

Thus, $F_{min} r = \tau$

$F_{min} r = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi i I r^2}{4 R}$

$F_{min} = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi i I r}{4 R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of curvature of a semicircular wire that has a radius of $10.0 \text{ cm}$ and carries a current of $5.0 \text{ A}$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a semicircular wire carrying current $I$ is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4r}$

Given:

$I = 5.0 \text{ A}$

$r = 10.0 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$

$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m/A}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0}{4 \times 0.1}$

$B = \dfrac{\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0}{0.1}$

$B = 50\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}$

$B = 5\pi \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$

Substituting $\pi = 3.14$:

$B = 5 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$

$B = 15.7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T}$

$B = 1.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Determine the magnetic field $B$ at the centre of a rectangular loop having length $l$ and width $b$, carrying a current $i$.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of the rectangular loop is the sum of the magnetic fields produced by its four sides.

The magnetic field due to a finite straight wire of length $L$ at a perpendicular distance $d$ from its centre is given by:

$B' = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi d} (\sin \theta_1 + \sin \theta_2)$

For the two sides of length $l$, the perpendicular distance to the centre is $d = \dfrac{b}{2}$. The angles are $\sin \theta_1 = \sin \theta_2 = \dfrac{l}{\sqrt{l^2 + b^2}}$.

The magnetic field due to these two sides is:

$B_1 = 2 \times \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi \left(\dfrac{b}{2}\right)} \left( \dfrac{2l}{\sqrt{l^2 + b^2}} \right) = \dfrac{2 \mu_0 i l}{\pi b \sqrt{l^2 + b^2}}$

For the two sides of length $b$, the perpendicular distance to the centre is $d = \dfrac{l}{2}$. The angles are $\sin \theta_1 = \sin \theta_2 = \dfrac{b}{\sqrt{l^2 + b^2}}$.

The magnetic field due to these two sides is:

$B_2 = 2 \times \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi \left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)} \left( \dfrac{2b}{\sqrt{l^2 + b^2}} \right) = \dfrac{2 \mu_0 i b}{\pi l \sqrt{l^2 + b^2}}$

The total magnetic field at the centre is the sum of $B_1$ and $B_2$ as they point in the same direction:

$B = B_1 + B_2 = \dfrac{2 \mu_0 i}{\pi \sqrt{l^2 + b^2}} \left( \dfrac{l}{b} + \dfrac{b}{l} \right)$

$B = \dfrac{2 \mu_0 i}{\pi \sqrt{l^2 + b^2}} \left( \dfrac{l^2 + b^2}{l b} \right) = \dfrac{2 \mu_0 i \sqrt{l^2 + b^2}}{\pi l b}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A circular loop with a radius of $20 \text{ cm}$ carries a $10 \text{ A}$ current. An electron passes through the centre of the loop, crossing its plane at a speed of $2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. If the electron's direction of motion is at a $30^\circ$ angle to the axis of the circle, determine the magnitude of the magnetic force acting on the electron at the moment it crosses the plane.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$

Substituting the given values:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10}{2 \times 0.2} = \pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

The direction of this magnetic field is along the axis of the circular loop.

The magnitude of the magnetic force acting on the electron is given by $F = qvB \sin\theta$

Substituting the values of charge, velocity, magnetic field, and angle:

$F = (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) \times (2.0 \times 10^6) \times (\pi \times 10^{-5}) \times \sin 30^\circ$

$F = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2.0 \times 10^6 \times \pi \times 10^{-5} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$

$F = 1.6\pi \times 10^{-18} \text{ N} = 16\pi \times 10^{-19} \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A piece of wire carrying a current of $6.00 \text{ A}$ is shaped into a circular arc of radius $10.0 \text{ cm}$. If the arc subtends an angle of $120^\circ$ at the centre, determine the magnetic field $B$ produced by this piece of wire at the centre.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular arc is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} \theta$

Given:
$I = 6.00 \text{ A}$
$r = 10.0 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
$\theta = 120^\circ = \dfrac{2\pi}{3} \text{ rad}$

Substituting the values:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 6.00}{4\pi \times 0.1} \times \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

$B = 10^{-6} \times 60 \times \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

$B = 40\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}$

$B = 4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T} = 1.256 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T} \approx 1.26 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A circular loop of radius $r$ carries a current $i$. How must a long, straight wire carrying a current $4i$ be positioned in the plane of the circle such that the magnetic field at the centre becomes zero?
options: At a distance of $\dfrac{4r}{\pi}$ from the centre, with its current direction opposite to that in the nearest part of the loop
At a distance of $\dfrac{2r}{\pi}$ from the centre, with its current direction the same as that in the nearest part of the loop
At a distance of $\dfrac{4r}{\pi}$ from the centre, with its current direction the same as that in the nearest part of the loop
At a distance of $\dfrac{2r}{\pi}$ from the centre, with its current direction opposite to that in the nearest part of the loop
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of the circular loop due to its own current $i$ is given by:

$B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2r}$

The magnetic field at the centre due to the long straight wire carrying current $4i$ at a distance $d$ is:

$B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 (4i)}{2\pi d}$

For the net magnetic field at the centre to be zero, the magnitudes of the magnetic fields must be equal and their directions must be opposite. Equating the magnitudes:

$\dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2r} = \dfrac{\mu_0 (4i)}{2\pi d}$

$d = \dfrac{4r}{\pi}$

For the fields to be opposite at the centre, the straight wire must produce a magnetic field in the opposite direction to that of the loop. By the right-hand thumb rule, if the straight wire is placed near the loop, its current must flow in the direction opposite to the current in the nearest part of the circular loop. This ensures that the magnetic field produced by the straight wire at the centre opposes the magnetic field produced by the loop.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A circular coil consisting of $200$ turns has a radius of $10 \text{ cm}$ and carries a current of $2.0 \text{ A}$. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ at the centre of the coil.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil of $N$ turns is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 N I}{2R}$

Given parameters:
$N = 200$
$R = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
$I = 2.0 \text{ A}$
$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}\cdot\text{m/A}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 200 \times 2.0}{2 \times 0.1}$

$B = \dfrac{1600\pi \times 10^{-7}}{0.2}$

$B = 8000\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}$

$B = 8\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$B = 8 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

$B = 25.12 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$

$B = 2.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T} = 2.51 \text{ mT}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A circular coil consisting of $200$ turns has a radius of $10 \text{ cm}$ and carries a current of $2.0 \text{ A}$. At what distance from the centre along the axis of the coil will the magnetic field drop to half of its value at the centre? (Take $\sqrt[3]{4} = 1.5874 \ldots$)
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a circular coil is given by $B_{c} = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{2 R}$

The magnetic field at a distance $x$ from the centre on its axis is given by $B_{x} = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N I R^{2}}{2 (R^{2} + x^{2})^{3/2}}$

Given that $B_{x} = \dfrac{B_{c}}{2}$

$\dfrac{\mu_{0} N I R^{2}}{2 (R^{2} + x^{2})^{3/2}} = \dfrac{\mu_{0} N I}{4 R}$

$\dfrac{R^{2}}{(R^{2} + x^{2})^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1}{2 R}$

$(R^{2} + x^{2})^{3/2} = 2 R^{3}$

Taking the power of $2/3$ on both sides:

$R^{2} + x^{2} = 2^{2/3} R^{2}$

$x^{2} = R^{2} (4^{1/3} - 1)$

$x = R \sqrt{4^{1/3} - 1}$

Substituting $R = 10 \text{ cm}$ and $4^{1/3} = 1.5874$:

$x = 10 \sqrt{1.5874 - 1}$

$x = 10 \sqrt{0.5874}$

$x \approx 10 \times 0.7664 = 7.664 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Situated in a horizontal plane, a circular loop of radius $4.0 \text{ cm}$ carries an electric current of $5.0 \text{ A}$ in the clockwise direction when viewed from above. Determine the magnetic field at a point $3.0 \text{ cm}$ above the centre of the loop.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at a distance $x$ on the axis of a circular loop of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

Given values are:
$I = 5.0 \text{ A}$
$R = 4.0 \text{ cm} = 4.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$
$x = 3.0 \text{ cm} = 3.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$
$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m/A}$

Calculating the denominator term:
$R^2 + x^2 = (4.0 \times 10^{-2})^2 + (3.0 \times 10^{-2})^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2} = (25 \times 10^{-4})^{3/2} = 125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

Substituting the values into the formula:
$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0 \times (16 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times 125 \times 10^{-6}}$

$B = \dfrac{320\pi \times 10^{-11}}{250 \times 10^{-6}}$

$B = 1.28\pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:
$B \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

Since the current is in the clockwise direction when viewed from above, applying the right-hand thumb rule (curling the fingers of the right hand in the direction of current makes the thumb point downwards) gives the direction of the magnetic field as downwards.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Situated in a horizontal plane, a circular loop of radius $4.0 \text{ cm}$ carries an electric current of $5.0 \text{ A}$ in the clockwise direction when viewed from above. Determine the magnetic field at a point $3.0 \text{ cm}$ below the centre of the loop.
questions.solution:
The magnitude of the magnetic field on the axis of a circular loop is given by:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + z^2)^{3/2}}$

Given values are:
$I = 5.0 \text{ A}$
$R = 4.0 \text{ cm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
$z = 3.0 \text{ cm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$
$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m/A}$

Substituting the values into the formula:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0 \times (0.04)^2}{2((0.04)^2 + (0.03)^2)^{3/2}}$

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2(1.6 \times 10^{-3} + 0.9 \times 10^{-3})^{3/2}}$

$B = \dfrac{32\pi \times 10^{-10}}{2(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^{3/2}}$

$B = \dfrac{32\pi \times 10^{-10}}{2(125 \times 10^{-6})}$

$B = \dfrac{32\pi \times 10^{-10}}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.28\pi \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$B \approx 1.28 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

According to the right-hand thumb rule, since the current is in the clockwise direction when viewed from above, the magnetic field lines enter the loop from above and exit from below. Thus, the direction of the magnetic field at any point on the axis is downwards.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two parallel wires separated by a distance of $10 \text{ cm}$ carry currents of $10 \text{ A}$ and $40 \text{ A}$ in the same direction. At what position should a third current be placed such that it experiences zero magnetic force?
options: $8 \text{ cm}$ from the $10 \text{ A}$ current, between the wires
$8 \text{ cm}$ from the $10 \text{ A}$ current, outside the wires
$2 \text{ cm}$ from the $10 \text{ A}$ current, between the wires
$2 \text{ cm}$ from the $10 \text{ A}$ current, outside the wires
questions.solution:
Let the third wire be placed at a distance $x$ from the $10 \text{ A}$ wire.

For the third wire to experience zero magnetic force, the net magnetic field at its position must be zero.

Since the currents in the two wires are in the same direction, the magnetic fields produced by them will be in opposite directions only in the region between the wires.

Equating the magnitudes of the magnetic fields produced by the two wires at distance $x$ from the $10 \text{ A}$ wire:

$B_1 = B_2$

$\dfrac{\mu_0 I_1}{2 \pi x} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_2}{2 \pi (d - x)}$

Substituting $I_1 = 10 \text{ A}$, $I_2 = 40 \text{ A}$, and $d = 10 \text{ cm}$:

$\dfrac{10}{x} = \dfrac{40}{10 - x}$

$10 - x = 4x$

$5x = 10$

$x = 2 \text{ cm}$

The third wire must be placed $2 \text{ cm}$ from the $10 \text{ A}$ current, between the wires.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A uniform charge of $3.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}$ is distributed over a circular ring having a radius of $20.0 \text{ cm}$. The ring is rotating about its axis with an angular velocity of $60.0 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$. Determine the ratio of the electric field to the magnetic field at a point located on the axis at a distance of $5.00 \text{ cm}$ from the centre.
questions.solution:
The electric field $E$ at a distance $x$ on the axis of a uniformly charged ring is given by:

$E = \dfrac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \dfrac{q x}{(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

The magnetic field $B$ at a distance $x$ on the axis of a circular current loop is:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i R^2}{2 (R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

The equivalent current $i$ due to the rotating charge is:

$i = \dfrac{q}{T} = \dfrac{q \omega}{2 \pi}$

Substituting $i$ into the expression for $B$:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 q \omega R^2}{4 \pi (R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$

Taking the ratio of $E$ to $B$:

$\dfrac{E}{B} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \dfrac{q x}{(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}}{\dfrac{\mu_0 q \omega R^2}{4 \pi (R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}} = \dfrac{x}{\epsilon_0 \mu_0 \omega R^2}$

Using the relation $c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\epsilon_0 \mu_0}}$, we get:

$\dfrac{E}{B} = \dfrac{x c^2}{\omega R^2}$

Given values are:
$x = 5.00 \text{ cm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
$R = 20.0 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
$\omega = 60.0 \text{ rad s}^{-1}$
$c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Substituting the values:

$\dfrac{E}{B} = \dfrac{0.05 \times (3 \times 10^8)^2}{60 \times (0.2)^2}$

$\dfrac{E}{B} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 9 \times 10^{16}}{60 \times 0.04}$

$\dfrac{E}{B} = \dfrac{0.45 \times 10^{16}}{2.4}$

$\dfrac{E}{B} = \dfrac{4.5}{24} \times 10^{16}$

$\dfrac{E}{B} = \dfrac{3}{16} \times 10^{16}$

$\dfrac{E}{B} = 0.1875 \times 10^{16} = 1.875 \times 10^{15} \text{ m s}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, thin, hollow cylindrical tube of radius $r$ carries a current $i$ along its length. Calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field at a distance $r/2$ from the surface, outside the tube.
questions.solution:
Let the distance of the point from the axis of the cylinder be $R$.

Since the point is at a distance $r/2$ from the surface outside the tube, we have $R = r + \dfrac{r}{2} = \dfrac{3r}{2}$.

By Ampere's circuital law, the magnetic field outside a long cylindrical tube carrying current $i$ is given by:

$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 i$

$B(2\pi R) = \mu_0 i$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi R}$

Substituting $R = \dfrac{3r}{2}$:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi \left(\dfrac{3r}{2}\right)} = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{3\pi r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, thin, hollow cylindrical tube of radius $r$ carries a current $i$ along its length. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field at a distance $r/2$ from the surface, inside the tube.
questions.solution:
Using Ampere's circuital law:

$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enclosed}$

For a point inside the hollow cylindrical tube at a distance $r/2$ from the surface, the distance from the axis is $r - r/2 = r/2$.

An Amperian loop of radius $r/2$ inside the hollow tube encloses no current, so $I_{enclosed} = 0$.

Therefore, $B \times 2\pi \left(\dfrac{r}{2}\right) = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$

The magnetic field at any point inside the hollow tube is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a square loop $PQRS$ carrying a current of $6.0\text{ A}$ is situated near a long wire carrying $10\text{ A}$. Determine the net magnetic force on the square loop.
questions.solution:
The magnetic force on a straight wire of length $L$ carrying current $I_2$ placed at a distance $r$ from a long straight wire carrying current $I_1$ is given by:

$F = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2 L}{2\pi r}$

The forces on the horizontal segments $PQ$ and $RS$ are equal and opposite, and their net force is zero.

For the side $SP$ of the square loop:
The current in $SP$ is downwards, which is anti-parallel to the current in the long wire. Therefore, the force on $SP$ is repulsive (towards the right).
Distance $r_1 = 1\text{ cm} = 0.01\text{ m}$
Length $L = 2\text{ cm} = 0.02\text{ m}$
$F_{SP} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10 \times 6 \times 0.02}{2\pi \times 0.01} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5}\text{ N}$ (towards right)

For the side $RQ$ of the square loop:
The current in $RQ$ is upwards, which is parallel to the current in the long wire. Therefore, the force on $RQ$ is attractive (towards the left).
Distance $r_2 = 1\text{ cm} + 2\text{ cm} = 3\text{ cm} = 0.03\text{ m}$
$F_{RQ} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 10 \times 6 \times 0.02}{2\pi \times 0.03} = 0.8 \times 10^{-5}\text{ N}$ (towards left)

The net magnetic force on the loop is:
$F_{net} = F_{SP} - F_{RQ} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} - 0.8 \times 10^{-5} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}\text{ N}$

Since $F_{SP} > F_{RQ}$, the net force is directed towards the right.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A square loop $PQRS$ carrying a current of $6.0\text{ A}$ is positioned near a long wire carrying $10\text{ A}$ such that sides $SP$ and $RQ$ are parallel to the wire. Which of the following statements correctly describes the magnetic forces acting on the segments $PQ$ and $RS$?
options: The force on $PQ$ is greater in magnitude than the force on $RS$.
The force on $RS$ is greater in magnitude than the force on $PQ$.
They are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.
They are equal in magnitude and point in the same direction.
questions.solution:
