Let the long straight wire lie along the y-axis, carrying current $I_1 = 10\text{ A}$ in the $+y$ direction.

The magnetic field $\vec{B}$ produced by this wire at a perpendicular distance $x$ is directed into the plane of the paper ($-z$ direction). Its magnitude is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi x}$.

For the segment $PQ$, the current $I_2 = 6.0\text{ A}$ flows away from the wire (in the $+x$ direction). The magnetic force on a small element $dx$ is:

$d\vec{F}_{PQ} = I_2 (dx\,\hat{i}) \times (-B\,\hat{k}) = I_2 B dx\,\hat{j}$

The total force on $PQ$ is directed upwards ($+y$ direction) and its magnitude is:

$F_{PQ} = \int_{1}^{3} I_2 \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi x}\right) dx = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi} \ln(3)$

For the segment $RS$, the current $I_2$ flows towards the wire (in the $-x$ direction). The magnetic force on a small element $dx$ is:

$d\vec{F}_{RS} = I_2 (-dx\,\hat{i}) \times (-B\,\hat{k}) = -I_2 B dx\,\hat{j}$

The total force on $RS$ is directed downwards ($-y$ direction) and its magnitude is:

$F_{RS} = \int_{1}^{3} I_2 \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 I_1}{2\pi x}\right) dx = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi} \ln(3)$

Thus, the forces on segments $PQ$ and $RS$ are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long cylindrical tube with inner and outer radii $a$ and $b$ carries a current $i$ that is distributed uniformly across its cross section. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field at a location just inside the tube.
questions.solution:
The current $i$ flows through the cross-section of the cylindrical tube between the inner radius $a$ and outer radius $b$.

For a point just inside the tube (in the hollow region), the radial distance $r$ from the central axis is $r \le a$.

Considering a circular Amperian loop of radius $r \le a$ concentric with the axis of the tube, the current enclosed by this loop is zero, as the current flows only in the region $a < r < b$.

According to Ampere's circuital law:

$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enclosed}}$

Since $I_{\text{enclosed}} = 0$, we get:

$B(2\pi r) = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$

Thus, the magnitude of the magnetic field at a location just inside the tube is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, cylindrical wire having radius $b$ carries a uniformly distributed current $i$ across its cross section. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field at a point situated inside the wire, at a distance $a$ from its axis.
questions.solution:
Using Ampere's circuital law: $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enclosed}$

For a point inside the wire at a distance $a$ from the axis, consider a circular Amperian loop of radius $a$.

Since the current is uniformly distributed over the cross-section of radius $b$, the current density is:

$J = \dfrac{i}{\pi b^2}$

The current enclosed by the Amperian loop is:

$I_{enclosed} = J \times \pi a^2 = \dfrac{i}{\pi b^2} \times \pi a^2 = \dfrac{i a^2}{b^2}$

Applying Ampere's law:

$B(2\pi a) = \mu_0 \left( \dfrac{i a^2}{b^2} \right)$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i a}{2\pi b^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A solid wire with a radius of $10 \text{ cm}$ carries a $5.0 \text{ A}$ current that is uniformly distributed across its cross section. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at a point located at a distance of $20 \text{ cm}$ from the axis.
questions.solution:
Using Ampere's circuital law, the magnetic field at a point outside a long straight current-carrying wire is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}$

Given that the point is at a distance $r = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$ from the axis, which is greater than the radius of the wire ($R = 10 \text{ cm}$), the point lies outside the wire.

Substituting the given values $I = 5.0 \text{ A}$ and $r = 0.2 \text{ m}$:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0}{2 \pi \times 0.2}$

$B = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0}{0.2}$

$B = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-7}}{0.2}$

$B = 50 \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}$

$B = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T} = 5.0 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A solid wire with a radius of $10 \text{ cm}$ carries a $5.0 \text{ A}$ current that is uniformly distributed across its cross section. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at a point located at a distance of $10 \text{ cm}$ from the axis.
questions.solution:
Using Ampere's circuital law, the magnetic field at a distance $r$ from the axis of a solid cylindrical wire of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}$ for $r \ge R$.

Here, the point is at a distance $r = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$, which is exactly on the surface of the wire ($R = 10 \text{ cm}$).

Substituting the given values:

$B = \dfrac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0}{2 \pi \times 0.1}$

$B = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0}{0.1}$

$B = 100 \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}$

$B = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T} = 10 \text{ } \mu\text{T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A solid wire with a radius of $10 \text{ cm}$ carries a $5.0 \text{ A}$ current that is uniformly distributed across its cross section. Determine the magnetic field $B$ at a point located at a distance of $2 \text{ cm}$ from the axis.
questions.solution:
Given:
Radius of the wire, $R = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
Current, $I = 5.0 \text{ A}$
Distance from the axis, $r = 2 \text{ cm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$

The magnetic field inside a solid cylindrical wire carrying a uniform current at a distance $r < R$ is given by Ampere's Law:

$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enclosed}}$

$B(2\pi r) = \mu_0 \left( \dfrac{I}{\pi R^2} \right) \pi r^2$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 I r}{2\pi R^2}$

Substituting the given values:

$B = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.0 \times 0.02}{2\pi \times (0.1)^2}$

$B = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.1}{0.01}$

$B = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ T} = 2.0 \text{ }\mu\text{T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two large metal sheets carry surface currents as depicted in the figure. The current through a strip of width $dl$ is given by $K \, dl$, where $K$ is a constant. A particle with charge $q$ and mass $m$ is projected from point $Q$ in a direction going into the plane of the diagram. It is observed to describe a circle of radius $r$ between the two plates. Determine the speed of the charged particle.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by a single large metal sheet carrying a surface current density $K$ is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$.

For the top sheet, the current is directed out of the plane of the paper. According to the right-hand rule, the magnetic field produced by the top sheet at point Q (which lies below it) is directed towards the right, with magnitude $B_1 = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$.

For the bottom sheet, the current is directed into the plane of the paper. The magnetic field produced by the bottom sheet at point Q (which lies above it) is also directed towards the right, with magnitude $B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$.

The net magnetic field at point Q is the vector sum of the fields from both sheets. Since they point in the same direction, the net magnetic field is:

$B_{net} = B_1 + B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2} + \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2} = \mu_0 K$

When a particle of charge $q$ and mass $m$ is projected with speed $v$ into a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity, it moves in a circular path. The magnetic force provides the necessary centripetal force:

$q v B_{net} = \dfrac{m v^2}{r}$

Solving for the speed $v$, we get:

$v = \dfrac{q B_{net} r}{m}$

Substituting the value of $B_{net}$:

$v = \dfrac{\mu_0 K q r}{m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: An idealized magnetic field that is uniform within a specific region and abruptly drops to zero is sometimes depicted, as illustrated in the figure. By applying Ampere's law along the path $PQRS$, identify the reason why such a field configuration is impossible.
options: Ampere's law is only applicable to non-uniform magnetic fields and cannot be evaluated along the chosen path.
The line integral $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$ over the loop $PQRS$ is exactly zero, whereas the enclosed current is non-zero.
The line integral $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$ over the loop $PQRS$ evaluates to a non-zero value, whereas the enclosed current is zero.
The line integral $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$ is mathematically undefined at the boundary where the magnetic field abruptly drops to zero.
questions.solution:
Consider the rectangular Amperian loop $PQRS$.

The line integral of the magnetic field along the loop is given by:

$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int_P^Q \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} + \int_Q^R \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} + \int_R^S \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} + \int_S^P \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l}$

Along the paths $PQ$ and $RS$, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is perpendicular to the path elements $d\vec{l}$, so $\vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$.

Along the path $QR$, the magnetic field is zero, so $\int_Q^R \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$.

Along the path $SP$, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is uniform and parallel to $d\vec{l}$. Let the length of $SP$ be $l$. Then $\int_S^P \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = Bl$.

Therefore, the total line integral is $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = Bl \neq 0$.

However, the current enclosed by the loop $PQRS$ is zero ($I_{enclosed} = 0$).

According to Ampere's law, $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enclosed}$. This would require $Bl = 0$, which contradicts the presence of a uniform magnetic field.

Thus, such a field configuration is impossible because the line integral evaluates to a non-zero value, whereas the enclosed current is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Inside a long solenoid that carries a current of $5.00 \text{ A}$, the magnetic field $B$ is found to be $3.14 \times 10^{-2} \text{ T}$. Determine the number of turns per unit length for this solenoid.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is given by the formula:

$B = \mu_{0} n I$

Rearranging for the number of turns per unit length $n$:

$n = \dfrac{B}{\mu_{0} I}$

Substituting the given values $B = 3.14 \times 10^{-2} \text{ T}$, $I = 5.00 \text{ A}$, and $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}\cdot\text{m/A}$:

$n = \dfrac{3.14 \times 10^{-2}}{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5.00}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$:

$n = \dfrac{3.14 \times 10^{-2}}{4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7} \times 5.00}$

$n = \dfrac{10^{-2}}{20 \times 10^{-7}}$

$n = \dfrac{10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$

$n = 0.5 \times 10^{4} = 5000 \text{ turns/m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A tightly-wound solenoid of radius $a$ and length $l$ has $n$ turns per unit length. It carries an electric current $i$. Consider a length $dx$ of the solenoid at a distance $x$ from one end, which contains $n \, dx$ turns and can be approximated as a circular current $i \, n \, dx$. Determine the total magnetic field at the centre of the solenoid by integrating the magnetic field due to this circular current under proper limits.
questions.solution:
Let the center of the solenoid be the origin. The distance of the elemental ring from the center is $y$.

The magnetic field at the center due to an elemental ring of width $dy$ at a distance $y$ from the center is:

$dB = \dfrac{\mu_0 i (n \, dy) a^2}{2(a^2 + y^2)^{3/2}}$

Integrating from $y = -\dfrac{l}{2}$ to $y = \dfrac{l}{2}$ to find the total magnetic field:

$B = \int_{-l/2}^{l/2} \dfrac{\mu_0 n i a^2}{2(a^2 + y^2)^{3/2}} dy$

Let $y = a \tan\theta$, then $dy = a \sec^2\theta \, d\theta$.

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i a^2}{2} \int \dfrac{a \sec^2\theta}{(a^2 \sec^2\theta)^{3/2}} d\theta = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{2} \int \cos\theta \, d\theta$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{2} [\sin\theta]$

Substituting $\sin\theta = \dfrac{y}{\sqrt{a^2 + y^2}}$ and applying the limits from $y = -\dfrac{l}{2}$ to $y = \dfrac{l}{2}$:

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{2} \left[ \dfrac{l/2}{\sqrt{a^2 + (l/2)^2}} - \dfrac{-l/2}{\sqrt{a^2 + (-l/2)^2}} \right]$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{2} \left( \dfrac{l}{\sqrt{a^2 + l^2/4}} \right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{2a}{l}\right)^2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long solenoid is constructed by tightly winding a wire of radius $0.5 \text{ mm}$ around a cylindrical nonmagnetic frame, ensuring that successive turns nearly touch one another. If the solenoid carries a current of $5 \text{ A}$, determine the magnetic field $B$ at its centre.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a long solenoid is given by $B = \mu_0 n I$, where $n$ is the number of turns per unit length.

Since the wire is tightly wound, the distance between successive turns is equal to the diameter of the wire.

Diameter of the wire $d = 2r = 2 \times 0.5 \text{ mm} = 1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.

The number of turns per unit length is $n = \dfrac{1}{d} = \dfrac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000 \text{ m}^{-1}$.

Substituting the values in the formula for magnetic field:

$B = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times 5$

$B = 20\pi \times 10^{-4} \text{ T} = 2\pi \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A tightly-wound solenoid of radius $a$ and length $l$ has $n$ turns per unit length and carries an electric current $i$. What does the magnetic field at the centre of the solenoid tend to in the limits $l \gg a$ and $a \gg l$, respectively?
options: $\mu_0 n i$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{2a}$
$\dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{2}$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{a}$
$\dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{2}$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{2a}$
$\mu_0 n i$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{a}$
questions.solution:
The magnetic field at the centre of a finite solenoid of length $l$ and radius $a$ having $n$ turns per unit length and carrying current $i$ is given by

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{2} (\sin \theta_1 + \sin \theta_2)$

where $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are the semi-vertical angles subtended by the ends at the centre.

At the centre of the solenoid, $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \theta$. From the geometry of the solenoid,

$\sin \theta = \dfrac{\dfrac{l}{2}}{\sqrt{a^2 + \left(\dfrac{l}{2}\right)^2}} = \dfrac{l}{\sqrt{4a^2 + l^2}}$

Substituting this into the expression for $B$,

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i}{2} \left( \dfrac{2l}{\sqrt{4a^2 + l^2}} \right) = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{\sqrt{4a^2 + l^2}}$

In the limit $l \gg a$, the term $4a^2$ can be neglected compared to $l^2$.

$B \rightarrow \dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{\sqrt{l^2}} = \mu_0 n i$

In the limit $a \gg l$, the term $l^2$ can be neglected compared to $4a^2$.

$B \rightarrow \dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{\sqrt{4a^2}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{2a}$

The magnetic field tends to $\mu_0 n i$ and $\dfrac{\mu_0 n i l}{2a}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: An electron is observed executing uniform circular motion with a frequency of $1.00 \times 10^8 \text{ rev s}^{-1}$ inside a tightly-wound, long solenoid. If the solenoid carries a current of $2.00 \text{ A}$, determine the number of turns per metre in the solenoid.
questions.solution:
The frequency of revolution of an electron in a magnetic field is given by $f = \dfrac{qB}{2\pi m}$.

The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is $B = \mu_0 n I$, where $n$ is the number of turns per unit length.

Substituting $B$ into the frequency formula, we get:

$f = \dfrac{q \mu_0 n I}{2\pi m}$

Rearranging for $n$:

$n = \dfrac{2\pi m f}{q \mu_0 I}$

Substituting the standard values $m = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m A}^{-1}$, and the given values $f = 1.00 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz}$, $I = 2.00 \text{ A}$:

$n = \dfrac{2\pi \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 2}$

$n = \dfrac{9.1 \times 10^{-23}}{6.4 \times 10^{-26}}$

$n = 1421.875 \approx 1420 \text{ turns m}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long, tightly-wound solenoid of radius $r$ has $n$ turns per unit length and carries a current $i$. A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ is projected from a point on the axis in a direction perpendicular to the axis. What is the maximum speed the particle can have such that it does not strike the solenoid?
questions.solution:
The magnetic field inside a long solenoid is uniform and directed along its axis.

$B = \mu_0 n i$

When a particle of charge $q$ and mass $m$ is projected from the axis perpendicular to the magnetic field with speed $v$, it moves in a circular path. The radius of this circular path is given by:

$R = \dfrac{mv}{qB}$

Since the particle is projected from the axis, the axis lies on the circumference of its circular trajectory. The maximum distance of the particle from the axis is equal to the diameter of its circular path, which is $2R$.

For the particle to not strike the solenoid, this maximum distance must be less than or equal to the radius of the solenoid $r$.

$2R \le r$

$2 \left( \dfrac{mv}{qB} \right) \le r$

$v \le \dfrac{qBr}{2m}$

Substituting the value of the magnetic field $B = \mu_0 n i$:

$v_{max} = \dfrac{\mu_0 q r n i}{2m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A tightly-wound, long solenoid is kept with its axis parallel to a large metal sheet carrying a surface current. The surface current through a width $dl$ of the sheet is $K \, dl$ and the number of turns per unit length of the solenoid is $n$. The magnetic field near the centre of the solenoid is found to be zero. Determine the current in the solenoid.
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by a large metal sheet carrying a surface current density $K$ is given by $B_{sheet} = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$.

The magnetic field inside a long solenoid with $n$ turns per unit length and carrying a current $I$ is given by $B_{solenoid} = \mu_0 n I$.

Since the net magnetic field near the centre of the solenoid is zero, the magnetic field due to the solenoid must be equal and opposite to the magnetic field due to the metal sheet.

Equating the magnitudes of the two magnetic fields:

$\mu_0 n I = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$

$I = \dfrac{K}{2n}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A capacitor with a capacitance of $100 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$ is connected to a $20\text{ V}$ battery for a long time before being disconnected. It is subsequently connected across a long solenoid that has $4000$ turns per metre. It is observed that the potential difference across the capacitor drops to $90\%$ of its maximum value in $2.0$ seconds. Determine the average magnetic field produced at the centre of the solenoid during this time interval.
questions.solution:
The initial charge on the capacitor is given by:

$Q_{i} = C V_{i} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 20 = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ C}$

After $2.0 \text{ s}$, the potential difference drops to $90\%$ of its maximum value. The new potential difference is:

$V_{f} = 0.9 \times 20 = 18 \text{ V}$

The charge on the capacitor at this time is:

$Q_{f} = C V_{f} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 18 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ C}$

The average current $\bar{I}$ flowing through the solenoid during this time interval $\Delta t = 2.0 \text{ s}$ is the rate of flow of charge:

$\bar{I} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{Q_{i} - Q_{f}}{\Delta t}$

$\bar{I} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3} - 1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0} = \dfrac{0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0} = 10^{-4} \text{ A}$

The magnetic field at the centre of a long solenoid is $B = \mu_{0} n I$. Therefore, the average magnetic field $\bar{B}$ is:

$\bar{B} = \mu_{0} n \bar{I}$

Substituting the given values ($n = 4000 \text{ m}^{-1}$ and $\mu_{0} = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T m/A}$):

$\bar{B} = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 4000 \times 10^{-4}$

$\bar{B} = 16\pi \times 10^{-8} = 1.6\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A long cylindrical tube with inner and outer radii $a$ and $b$ carries a current $i$ that is distributed uniformly across its cross section. Determine the magnitude of the magnetic field at a location just outside the tube.
questions.solution:
Using Ampere's circuital law:

$\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I_{enclosed}$

For a point just outside the cylindrical tube, the radial distance from the axis is $r = b$.

Considering a circular Amperian loop of radius $r = b$ coaxial with the tube, the total current enclosed by this loop is the entire current $i$ carried by the tube.

$B (2\pi b) = \mu_0 i$

$B = \dfrac{\mu_0 i}{2\pi b}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: Two large metal sheets carry surface currents as depicted in the figure. The current through a strip of width $dl$ is $K \, dl$, where $K$ is a constant. Determine the magnetic field at the points $P$, $Q$, and $R$.
options: $\mu_0 K$ towards left at $P$, $0$ at $Q$, $\mu_0 K$ towards right at $R$
$\dfrac{1}{2}\mu_0 K$ towards right at $P$, $\mu_0 K$ towards right at $Q$, $\dfrac{1}{2}\mu_0 K$ towards right at $R$
$0$ at $P$, $\mu_0 K$ towards right at $Q$, $0$ at $R$
$0$ at $P$, $\mu_0 K$ towards left at $Q$, $0$ at $R$
questions.solution:
The magnetic field due to a large infinite metal sheet carrying a surface current density $K$ is uniform and its magnitude is given by $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$.

For the top sheet, the current is coming out of the plane of the paper. By the right-hand thumb rule, the magnetic field produced by it is directed towards the left above the sheet and towards the right below the sheet.

For the bottom sheet, the current is going into the plane of the paper. The magnetic field produced by it is directed towards the right above the sheet and towards the left below the sheet.

At point $P$ (above both sheets):
The field due to the top sheet is $\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$ towards the left.
The field due to the bottom sheet is $\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$ towards the right.
Net magnetic field at $P = 0$.

At point $Q$ (between the sheets):
The field due to the top sheet is $\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$ towards the right.
The field due to the bottom sheet is $\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$ towards the right.
Net magnetic field at $Q = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2} + \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2} = \mu_0 K$ towards the right.

At point $R$ (below both sheets):
The field due to the top sheet is $\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$ towards the right.
The field due to the bottom sheet is $\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$ towards the left.
Net magnetic field at $R = 0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A tightly-wound, long solenoid is initially kept with its axis parallel to a large metal sheet carrying a surface current. The surface current through a width $dl$ of the sheet is $K \, dl$ and the number of turns per unit length of the solenoid is $n$. In this initial orientation, the magnetic field near the centre of the solenoid is found to be zero. If the solenoid is subsequently rotated to make its axis perpendicular to the metal sheet, what would be the magnitude of the magnetic field near its centre?
questions.solution:
The magnetic field produced by a large metal sheet carrying a surface current density $K$ is uniform and parallel to the sheet. By Ampere's law, its magnitude is given by:

$B_{\text{sheet}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$

Initially, the solenoid's axis is parallel to the sheet, and the net magnetic field at its centre is zero. This implies that the magnetic field produced by the solenoid exactly cancels the magnetic field of the sheet. Therefore, the magnetic field of the solenoid must be equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the field of the sheet:

$B_{\text{sol}} = B_{\text{sheet}} = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}$

When the solenoid is rotated such that its axis is perpendicular to the metal sheet, its magnetic field $B_{\text{sol}}$ becomes perpendicular to the sheet. However, the magnetic field of the sheet $B_{\text{sheet}}$ remains parallel to the sheet.

Since the two magnetic fields are now perpendicular to each other, the magnitude of the resultant magnetic field near the centre of the solenoid is the vector sum of the two fields:

$B_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{B_{\text{sol}}^2 + B_{\text{sheet}}^2}$

Substituting the values, we get:

$B_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}\right)^2}$

$B_{\text{net}} = \sqrt{2 \left(\dfrac{\mu_0 K}{2}\right)^2} = \dfrac{\mu_0 K}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Magnetic Field due to a Current
questions.q: A copper wire with a resistance of $0.01 \text{ ohm}$ per metre is utilized to wind a $400$-turn solenoid having a radius of $1.0 \text{ cm}$ and a length of $20 \text{ cm}$. Determine the emf of a battery which, when connected across the solenoid, will produce a magnetic field of $1.0 \times 10^{-2} \text{ T}$ near the centre of the solenoid.
questions.solution:
The total length of the wire used to wind the solenoid is given by:

$l = N \times 2 \pi r = 400 \times 2 \pi \times 0.01 = 8 \pi \text{ m}$

The total resistance of the solenoid is:

$R = l \times \lambda = 8 \pi \times 0.01 = 0.08 \pi \text{ } \Omega$

The magnetic field near the centre of a long solenoid is:

$B = \mu_0 n I = \mu_0 \left( \dfrac{N}{L} \right) I$

Substituting the given values to find the current $I$:

$1.0 \times 10^{-2} = (4 \pi \times 10^{-7}) \times \left( \dfrac{400}{0.2} \right) \times I$

$1.0 \times 10^{-2} = 8 \pi \times 10^{-4} \times I$

$I = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-2}}{8 \pi \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{100}{8 \pi} = \dfrac{12.5}{\pi} \text{ A}$

The emf of the battery required is:

$E = I R = \left( \dfrac{12.5}{\pi} \right) \times 0.08 \pi = 1.0 \text{ V}$
